observations.recommendation:
We reiterated the previous years’ audit recommendations that:
1. the Municipal Accountant:
a. cause the immediate preparation and submission of the monthly BRS
for the two bank accounts under the General Fund for CYs 2021 to
2024:
b. prepare the necessary journal entries to take up all valid reconciling
items in the books of accounts;
c. thoroughly prepare/review the BRS to ensure that the reports are free
from errors and are provided with all the necessary details; and
d. in coordination with the Municipal Treasurer, exert diligent efforts in
tracing the records to identify the unreconciled balances and record
the necessary adjustments; and
e. henceforth, observe the timely preparation and submission of the BRS
in accordance with COA Circular No. 96-011; and
2. the Municipal Treasurer:
a. notify the payees of the stale checks to return them for cancellation and
replacement, if warranted, and inform the Municipal Accountant
that those stale checks are subject for adjustment in the books of
accounts; and
b. strictly adhere to the provisions of Sections 62(F) and 63(F) of the
LTOM by regularly updating and reconciling the cashbooks with the
bank statements and accounting subsidiary records at least once per
month or as the need arises.

We recommended that:
1. the Municipal Treasurer provide the Municipal Accountant at the
beginning of each year a certified list of all real property taxpayers and the
amount due and collectible for the year pursuant to Section 20 of the
MNGAS for LGUs; and
2. the Municipal Accountant (i) record the RPT/SET receivables each year
based on the Municipal Treasurer’s certified list of all real property
taxpayers for that year; and (ii) regularly reconcile the balances of RPT
and SET Receivable accounts per books with the amount of real property
tax delinquencies per records of the Treasury Office, to ensure the fair
presentation of those accounts in the financial statements.

We reiterated the previous year’s audit recommendations that the Municipal
Accountant: (i) trace the accounting records to determine the cause/s of the
discrepancies between the Intra-Agency Receivable and Payable accounts and
prepare the necessary journal entries to correct and reconcile the balances of these
accounts; and (ii) henceforth, thoroughly review the accounting entries in the JEVs
to ensure the accuracy of account balances in the financial statements.

We also recommended that the Municipal Treasurer exercise more caution in the
issuance of checks and official receipts and in depositing the collections to the
proper accounts so that the transactions may be recorded accordingly by the
Accounting Office.

We recommended that:
1. the Inventory Committee conduct a complete annual physical inventory
of PPE, including those properties recorded under the Special Education
Fund;
2. the Municipal Accountant (a) prepare the necessary journal entries to
derecognize the PPE items below the ₱50,000.00 capitalization threshold
and henceforth observe consistent application of appropriate accounting
policies when recognizing an item as PPE; and (b) reconcile the accounting
records with the RPCPPE to reflect accurate balances of PPE accounts in
the financial statements; and
3. the Municipal Treasurer, in her capacity as the General Services Officer,
coordinate with the Municipal Accountant and immediately establish and
maintain the Property Cards pursuant to Section 119 of the Manual on
the NGAS for LGUs;

We reiterated the previous year’s audit recommendations that:
1. the Municipal Accountant (a) trace the accounting records to determine
the nature and sources of the unidentified balances and the cause/s of
negative balances and make the necessary corrections in the subsidiary
ledgers for the Due to NGAs account; and (b) prepare the adjusting journal
entry to correct the account classification of funds for HEA; and
2. the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer (a) immediately
return to the source agencies the unutilized balances of the completed
projects and programs totaling ₱2,877,678.82; and (b) henceforth, observe
59
the timely revertion of all unutilized funds which are held in trust by the
LGU upon completion of their intended purpose pursuant to Item 4.9 of
COA Circular No. 94-013.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant determine the correct
composition of the reported balance of the TL-DRRMF account categorized by year
of transfer and by source agency and then, prepare the necessary journal entries to
correctly establish and maintain henceforth the proper subsidiary ledgers following
the account categorization prescribed under Section 5.1.12 of COA Circular No.
2012-002.

We reiterated the previous year’s recommendations that the:

a. cause the immediate preparation and submission of the complete set of monthly BRS on GF bank accounts for CYs 2021 to 2023, accompanied by a written justification for the three-year delay;
b. exert diligent efforts to review and analyze the unrecorded reconciling items, the abnormal negative balances and the unreconciled SL and then, record the necessary adjustments to the proper accounts, if warranted; and
c. henceforth, observe timely preparation and submission of the BRS in accordance with COA Circular No. 96-011 and ensure that all lawful financial transactions and adjustments are correctly recorded in the books of accounts within the period in which they were incurred; and
2. Municipal Treasurer notify the payees of the stale checks to return them for cancellation and replacement, if warranted; otherwise, inform the Municipal Accountant that the said checks are subject for adjustment in the books of accounts in accordance with Section 97 of PD No. 1445.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the:

Secretary to the SB (i) submit to the SP copies of duly approved ordinances within three (3) days after its approval; (ii) faithfully and impartially discharge all duties required of her by the law; and (iii) submit a satisfactory written explanation why the Appropriation Ordinances approving the CY 2020 Supplemental Budgets were not submitted to the SP for review and why no disciplinary action should be instituted against her for neglect of duty;

LCE (i) ensure that all phases in the local budget process are followed and complied with; (ii) monitor the status of Appropriation Ordinances for review by the SP; (iii) immediately request and obtain from the SB Secretary a copy of the approved appropriation ordinance duly received and signed by the SP Secretary or by its Presiding Officer as proof of receipt by the reviewing authority; and (iv) ensure that all municipal executive officials and employees faithfully discharge their duties and functions as provided by law and the LGC and institute administrative or judicial proceedings against any local official or employee who may have committed an offense in the performance of his/her official duties; and

Municipal Treasurer, as the official in charge of the disbursement of all local government funds, monitor the status of approved Appropriation Ordinances under review by coordinating with the LCE on the result or any action on the ordinance by the SP to ensure that all disbursements of funds from any of the items of appropriation therein are valid.

We further recommended that all the key officials in the budget process (i) take responsibility for their duties; (ii) create effective communication to avoid omitting necessary procedures required by the law; and (iii) remind everyone that approved ordinances, although already in effect and authorized for execution, is still subject for review by the reviewing authority pursuant to Section 327 of the LGC.

We recommended that the:
Municipal Mayor (i) exercise prudence in seeing that only those travels which purpose are essential to the needs of the office or the presence of the official/employee is critical to the outcome of the meeting, conference, seminar, or any official activity and the projected expenses are not excessive are approved; and (ii) instruct the officers and employees concerned to:
see to it that miscellaneous expenses and hotel accommodations do not exceed the allowed limit of ₱1,500.00 per night on top of full DTE and reduce the amount of DTE when the seminar, conference and/or workshops registration fee already included provisions for meals and accommodations;
compress travel period especially when the sole purpose of travel is to submit documents to various government agencies in PPC and avoid having travel companion for travels that can be done singlehandedly to save time and DTE;
provide specific travel purpose in the travel order and stop using generic travel purpose such as Transact Official Business; and
maximize the use of the MGA liaison office in PPC by forwarding various documents and reports and let the liaison officer submit/transmit the documents to the different offices to save travelling expenses in accordance with Section 2 of PD No. 1445.
OIC-Municipal Accountant, as internal auditor of the MGA, scrutinize the regularity and completeness of supporting documents for travelling expenses following the guidelines of EO No. 77 and DILG MC No.  2019-82 and see to it that DTE in the itineraries of travel are reviewed, checked and corrected to ensure reasonableness of the claims and to prevent the incurrence of irregular, unnecessary and excessive expenditures.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, instruct the OIC-HRMO, OIC-Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to:
review the loan contracts of all officers and employees before certifying the agreement to withhold loan payments from their salaries to ensure that no employee will receive net take-home pay below ₱5,000.00 in strict compliance to the provisions of the GAA; and
instruct those officers and employees with less than ₱5,000.00 net take-home pay to directly remit their payments to the collecting agents/office of their creditors.

SB Secretary (i) submit to the SP copies of duly approved ordinances within three (3) days after its approval; (ii) faithfully and impartially discharge all duties required of her by the law; and (iii) submit a satisfactory written explanation why the Appropriation Ordinances approving the CY 2020 Supplemental Budgets were not submitted to the SP for review and why no disciplinary action should be instituted against her for neglect of duty;

We further recommended that all the key officials in the budget process (i) take responsibility for their duties; (ii) create effective communication to avoid omitting necessary procedures required by the law; and (iii) remind everyone that approved ordinances, although already in effect and authorized for execution, is still subject for review by the reviewing authority pursuant to Section 327 of the LGC.
The Management has not commented on the foregoing audit observation.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
carefully (i) examine the effect and limitations of reassigning officers and employees particularly of key officials by reviewing the existing internal control that might be affected by the movement to ensure that there is proper segregation of duties to prevent irregularities and violation of existing laws, rules and regulations; and (ii) consider the merits and relevance of reassigning employees to avoid unnecessary transfer that could be disadvantageous in the Municipality’s pursuit for good governance; and
enforce Executive Order No. 6 by directing the inventory committee to conduct a complete physical count of inventories and property, plant and equipment as required by COA Circular No. 92-386 and other rules and regulations on Supply and Property Management and subsequently submit the corresponding RPCI and RPCPPE to the Audit Team and to the Municipal Accountant for reconciliation with accounting records.
The Management commented that they adhered to the proper segregation of duties such that on March 30, 2020, the BAC was reorganized upon reassignment of the BAC Chairperson/MBO as OIC-MA. Likewise, an OIC-MBO was designated. The Management also argued that there was a timing difference in these transactions since the DVs were not prepared until the completion of all the supporting documents. Hence, the DVs signed by the OIC-MA were supported by documents which he also signed as the BAC Chairperson, which was before his designation as OIC-MA.
Auditor’s Rejoinder
The concept behind the segregation of duties is to prevent a single individual from performing two or more tasks including authorization/approval and recording to prevent error and fraud since it will require two or more people to collude in order to conceal errors and/or illegal transactions. The AT acknowledged the reorganization of the BAC prior to the designation of the BAC Chairperson as OIC-MA, however, the existing conflict of interest and self-review should have been put into consideration. This was not the case on the 17 identified DVs since these were signed by the OIC-MA when he was still the MBO and at the same time pre-audited the supporting documents which were prepared by the BAC when he was also its Chairperson.
About the timing difference, if the decision of the management to relieve or reassign employees right away, without considering the outcome and its possible negative effect will be accepted as proper procedure, then it will be a leeway to circumvent the internal control principle and applicable rules and regulations which could be prone to abuse. As it was in this case, the purpose for which the internal control is installed was defeated, particularly in the segregation of duties. The Management could have waited until after all the transactions of the former BAC Chairperson have been settled before designating him as OIC-MA. Moreover, the BAC reorganization, the reassignment of BAC Chairperson/MBO as OIC-MA, and the MA to take sole responsibility in conducting the complete inventory of all the municipal properties and disposal lacked justifiable reason.

We recommended that the Local School Board:
prioritize the formulation of the SEF Budget by preparing and approving the SEF Budget within the prescribed period pursuant to Section 5 of DepEd-DILG JC No. 1, s. 2017; and
convene a meeting especially during the formulation of the SEF Budget even without the presence of the LSB Chairperson so long as majority of all the members constitute a quorum with the Co-Chair as the presiding officer.
In her written reply, the LCE explained that the non-preparation of the CY 2020 SEF Budget before the year was due of some political issues which caused confusion and affected various office transactions in the Municipality. In addition, the surge of the COVID-19 in the Municipality prevented the concerned officials to convene for a meeting and constitute a quorum. Nevertheless, she committed that the audit recommendations will be taken seriously and will be implemented.
Auditor’s Rejoinder
The delay in the LSB’s approval of the CY 2020 SEF Budget could not be attributed to the reasons cited by the Management. The COVID-19 pandemic only started in March 2020, thus did not affect activities conducted in the year 2019. On the other hand, if there were indeed confusion due to political issues, the District Supervisor of Schools, who is a co-chairperson of the LSB, should have called for a meeting to review and approve the said budget before the start of the year 2020. Most of the members of the LSB are not elected officials, which should allow them to perform their duties amid political issues.
The requirement that the SEF annual budget be prepared and approved before the year of its effectivity must be viewed by the LSB with importance as this directly affects its execution.  Early approval and proper implementation of the budget will also allow the board to make timely adjustments to maximize the use of the fund.

We recommended that the (a) Municipal Accountant (i) trace, locate and recover the documents related to the above accounts, prepare a reconciliation thereof and record the details obtained in the respective subsidiary ledgers; and (ii) submit the required schedule of subsidiary ledger balances of the controlling accounts to the Audit Team to facilitate verification and audit of accounts and the financial statements; and (b) Municipal Mayor ensure that the Municipal Accountant maintains the required books of accounts including subsidiary ledgers pursuant to Section 114(2) of P.D. 1445.

We reiterated our recommendation that the (a) Municipal Mayor, thru the Municipal Accountant, regularly serve written notice to accountable officers and employees demanding immediate settlement of their outstanding cash advances and institute the necessary legal action against those who failed to settle their accountabilities without justifiable cause; and (b) Municipal Accountant exert more efforts in retrieving prior years’ accounting records to itemize the unidentified balances amounting to ₱535,462.84 so that proper adjusting entries may be taken up in the books.
In all instances, the Municipal Mayor and the Municipal Accountant ensure that the provisions of COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997 are strictly observed and that all cash advances are fully liquidated at the end of the year.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor require the Inventory Committee to complete the annual physical count of PPE, reconcile the results of the count with the property and accounting records, and submit the RPCPPE to the Audit Team, pursuant to Section 124 of the Manual on the NGAS for LGUs, Volume I.  All discrepancies between physical and book inventories must be investigated, cleared and reconciled immediately.  If necessary, written explanation should be required from accountable and responsible person. He should likewise ensure that physical inventory is undertaken annually and reports are submitted accordingly.

Also, the Property Officer should ensure that all issued PPE for official use of officials and employees are covered by ARE which shall be renewed every January of the third year after issue.

We recommended that the:

We recommended that the:

No. 9184; and (iv) be reminded that concerned officials can be held personally liable for the amount for failing to take necessary measures to ensure that the project is implemented in accordance with the approved plans and schedules;
Municipal Engineer submit a revised Bar Chart/S-curve showing the projected and actual physical accomplishments as of the contract expiry dates of April 14, 2018 for the “Upgrading of Poblacion Earth Road to PCCP” project to facilitate the computation of the total liquidated damages to be imposed; and
Municipal Engineer provide an updated Status of Work Accomplished for each project in order to facilitate monitoring, validation and follow up inspection.

and local budget preparation forms prescribed under the Budget  Operations
Manual for LGUs and for the Sangguniang  Bayan  to  require,  prior  to  the
approval of the  Annual  and  Supplemental  Budgets,  that  special  purpose
appropriations are categorized by object of expenditure and presented  in  a
similar sequence as a department or office expenditure  program  except  the
staffing and compensation profile where there are no  personnel  and  office
to operate.

We recommended for the SWM Board to hasten the preparation of the 10-
year SWM Plan and the LGU to ensure  the  establishment  of  MRF  in  every
barangay, in accordance with R.A. No. 9003 and its implementing  rules  and
regulations for the furtherance of the objectives of the Act.

We recommended for the  Municipal  Mayor  to  instruct  the  Municipal
Treasurer to immediately transfer to a special trust fund the CYs  2011-
2016 LDRRMF unexpended  balance  totaling  ₱5,647,133.70  and  the  OIC-Municipal
Accountant to record said transfer in the account  “Trust  Liabilities-DRRM”
under the trust fund books, pursuant to Section 21 of  R.A.  No.  10121  and
Section 5.1.10 of COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012.


      The OIC-Municipal Accountant should also maintain  subsidiary  ledgers
for “Trust  Liabilities-DRRM”  and  reclassify  the  previously  transferred
LDRRMF unexpended  balance  in  the  amount  of  ₱1,059,999.00  from  “Other
Payables” to “Trust Liabilities-DRRM” account under the trust fund books.


      We further recommended for the LDRRM Officer to  observe  the  monthly
preparation and submission of the required reports, in compliance with the
provisions of the aforementioned regulations.

We recommended for the Municipal Mayor to ensure  that  the  programs,
projects and activities (PPAs) under the 20% Development Fund  are  strictly
in accordance with the provisions of DILG-DBM JMC  No.  2011-1  dated  April
13, 2011. The Municipal Development  Council,  the  Municipal  Planning  and
Development Coordinator, and the Municipal Budget Officer should  strictly
observe that the appropriations for the said fund are  restricted  to  those
priority development projects  identified  under  the  said  regulation  and
supported  with  identified,  detailed  and  specific  PPAs  in  the  Annual
Investment Plan.

We recommended for the members of the GFPS to strictly adhere with the
provisions  of  PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA  JMC  No.  2013-01  in  the   preparation,
attribution and allocation of GPB. The GFPS  should  also  ensure  that  all
recommendations of the DILG Regional Office in  the  review  of  the  GPB  are
properly observed before incorporating the same in the Annual Budget.

      Lastly, proper training and  seminar  should  be  intensified  to  the
members of the GFPS relative  to  GAD  concepts,  principles and policies,
especially on GAD planning, budgeting and HGDG  tool  to  enhance  and  broaden
their knowledge and proper execution of GAD PPAs.

We recommended for the Municipal Treasurer to immediately deposit the collections and unutilized amount of withdrawals as of December 31, 2015 and to henceforth comply with the requirement of depositing all collections intact to the authorized government depository bank as prescribed in Section 62 of the Local Treasury Operations Manual to avoid possible loss or misapplication of funds.

Also, the Municipal Treasurer should reconcile her cashbook balances with the accounting records so that discrepancies or errors could be immediately identified and corrected.

We further recommended that management should observe the provisions of COA Circular No. 97-002 which prescribes that payments should be made by check except in difficult, impractical or impossible situations. Cash payments shall only be made through a cash advance by a duly designated disbursing officer.
observations.reason: “To ensure full implementation, this observation is reiterated in page 47-50 of this AAR.”

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant exercise due professional care and diligence in the preparation of accounting worksheets, trial balances and financial statements, and review them thoroughly to ensure that they are free from material errors upon finalization.

We recommended for the immediate submission of the monitoring report on the municipal government’s projects/programs/activities as of December 31, 2015 containing details as to project name, location, total cost, date started, extensions, target and actual completion date, project status and total cost incurred to date. Henceforth, we recommended strict compliance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2013-004 dated January 30, 2013 as amended by COA Circular No. 2015-006 dated August 20, 2015.

We recommended that the management strictly adhere to the provisions of the Republic Act No. 9184 and its Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations on the procurement and implementation of infrastructure projects.

We also recommended that the management cause the submission of all the lacking supporting documents pertaining to the project. Henceforth, the Municipal Accountant must ensure completeness of supporting documents for infrastructure projects before any payment to the contractor is made.

We recommended that the GAD Focal Point System (GFPS) ensure strict adherence to PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-1 and COA Circular No. 2014-001 dated March 18, 2014 in the preparation of Annual GAD Plan and Budget (GPB) and Accomplishment Report (AR) and the submission thereof to concerned agencies. We also recommended for the GFPS to ensure that the Annual GPB was approved by the DILG before incorporating the same in the Annual Budget. The audit team should also be furnished with the GPB and AR as references in audit.
